children.name: Acrocephalus
children.description: Reed warblers — quintessential summer birds of Swedish reedbeds, combining remarkable vocal complexity with cryptic brown plumage and elusive habits that make them far more often heard than seen. The family name derives from Greek for 'pointed head', reflecting the flattened crown that gives these birds a characteristic profile as they slip through reed stems. The reed warbler's low, churring, repetitive song — mixing its own phrases with imitations of neighbouring species — emerges continuously from dense reedbeds all summer. The great reed warbler (trastsångare) is the largest European warbler and its powerful, frog-like song can drown out virtually everything else at a large Swedish reedbed. The marsh warbler is perhaps the most extraordinary of all: overwintering in Africa, it learns to mimic the calls of dozens of African species, incorporating over 200 different bird sounds into its astonishing vocal performance.

children.name: Acrocephalus scirpaceus
children.description: The rörsångare — Sweden's most familiar reedbed warbler, its churring 'chrrr-chrrr-churr-churr' song the defining sound of Swedish phragmites reedbeds from May through July. Builds a deep, cylindrical nest woven tightly around several reed stems — one of the most elegant bird nests in Sweden, capable of tilting 45 degrees without losing eggs. A preferred host of the cuckoo in southern Sweden. Arrives in May from sub-Saharan Africa; departs in August. Plumage is uniform warm brown above with a pale supercilium; identification primarily by voice and habitat.

children.name: Acrocephalus arundinaceus
children.description: The trastsångare — Europe's largest warbler, the size of a song thrush, with a powerful, carrying song that sounds like a mechanical frog crossed with a small bird. Its extraordinarily loud, repetitive 'karra-karra-karra-gurruk-gurruk' can be heard hundreds of metres from a reedbed. Scarce but annual breeding species in the largest Swedish reedbeds, particularly in Skåne and around Lake Mälaren. Males hold territories up to 2,000 m² within the reedbed and may attract several females simultaneously, making it one of Sweden's most polygynous songbirds.

children.name: Acrocephalus palustris
children.description: The kärrsångare — one of the world's most accomplished vocal mimics, incorporating the songs and calls of over 200 species into its continuous, churring performance — the majority of which are African birds learned on its wintering grounds in eastern Africa. Ornithologists can trace a marsh warbler's winter range by listening to which African species it has learned to imitate. Breeds in tall herbaceous vegetation — nettles, willowherb, meadowsweet — rather than reedbeds. Arrives very late in Sweden (late May to June) and is one of the last warblers to depart in August.

children.name: Locustella
children.description: Grasshopper warblers — skulking, mouse-like warblers defined by their extraordinary insect-like reeling songs that are produced by a specialised syrinx capable of generating a continuous, high-pitched trill at over 40 notes per second. The song of the grasshopper warbler (gräshoppsångare) is so convincingly insect-like that many people walk past singing males believing they are hearing a cricket or grasshopper. The birds themselves are almost never seen, creeping through dense low vegetation with their tail cocked and head low. They can rotate their head while singing, causing the volume to wax and wane as if the sound is moving. The grasshopper warbler is a declining breeding species in Sweden, affected by loss of overgrown young plantation, scrubby marshland edges, and other transitional habitats.

children.name: Locustella naevia
children.description: The gräshoppsångare — one of Sweden's most cryptic and elusive summer visitors. Its reeling, insect-like song — a continuous 'srrrrrrrr' lasting minutes without pause — is produced mainly at dawn and dusk and sounds indistinguishable from a bush cricket to the uninitiated. Streaked brown above with a spotted breast; creeps through low dense vegetation like a mouse and virtually never flies unless flushed. Breeds in overgrown young forestry, marsh edges, and tall coastal heathland in southern and central Sweden. Arrives in May; sings through June before becoming largely silent and invisible.

children.name: Hippolais
children.description: Hippolais warblers — medium-sized, active warblers of scrub and garden habitats with loud, complex songs that include mimicry of other species. The icterine warbler (härmsångare) is the most familiar member of the genus in Sweden: a yellow-green bird with a distinctively angular, peaked crown that breeds in parks, gardens, and mixed woodland edge throughout the country. Its song is a rich, tumbling medley of musical phrases, harsh notes, and imitated sounds delivered with great energy from prominent perches — one of the most complex and attractive warbler songs in Sweden. The Swedish name härmsångare ('mimic singer') perfectly captures the bird's talent for weaving snatches of other species' songs into its own performance. It is a short-distance migrant arriving late in May and departing early in August.

children.name: Hippolais icterina
children.description: The härmsångare — a striking yellow-green warbler with a peaked crown and large bill, one of the most musically accomplished summer visitors to Swedish gardens and parks. Its loud, chaotic song mixes melodious phrases with harsh rattles and skilled imitations of other species — from spotted flycatcher to chaffinch — in a continuous, bubbling performance. Breeds widely in Sweden from Skåne to central Norrland in open woodland, parks, and shrubby gardens. Closely related to the melodious warbler, which replaces it in southwestern Europe and occasionally wanders to Scandinavia.
